summary: Eight newly surfaced, comment-endorsed tools, systems, and a book from the 2026-07-18 HN front page.
telegram_footer: 8 items surfaced | 65/157 stories scraped (92 unreachable) | history + run artifact updated

items.name: cjpegli
items.summary: A JPEG encoder from the libjxl project, praised for high-quality progressive 4:4:4 compression.
items.why_included: A commenter gave clear first-hand approval and a concrete compression-quality rationale.
items.evidence: A commenter said they use cjpegli and that it “compresses best” with its default progressive, full-4:4:4 approach.

items.name: Solaar
items.source_story_title: LG monitors silently install software through Windows Update without consent
items.summary: A Linux manager for Logitech wireless devices, praised for exposing full Bolt-receiver features.
items.why_included: The recommendation was grounded in long-term personal use of Logitech hardware on Linux.
items.evidence: A commenter called Logitech MX hardware “rock solid” and said the Bolt receiver works great with Linux via Solaar.

items.name: curl_cffi
items.source_story_title: Show HN: Get alerts for good seats at 70mm IMAX showings of The Odyssey
items.summary: A Python HTTP client with curl-style browser impersonation, recommended for difficult web requests.
items.why_included: A commenter named it as their first library choice for the concrete Cloudflare-access problem.
items.evidence: A commenter wrote that curl_cffi is “the first library I reach for” when dealing with that situation.

items.name: Logitech MX Keys Mini
items.source_story_title: LG monitors silently install software through Windows Update without consent
items.summary: A compact wireless keyboard, praised as durable in multi-PC Linux use.
items.why_included: It received explicit long-term, first-hand approval in a discussion of peripheral software bloat.
items.evidence: A commenter said their MX Keys Mini, MX Anywhere mice, and trackballs are “all rock solid.”

items.name: Moonshine Micro
items.source_story_title: Speech Recognition and TTS in less than 500kb
items.summary: A sub-500 KB speech-recognition and text-to-speech runtime for constrained devices.
items.why_included: The project received direct positive reaction and was noted as useful for fast Raspberry Pi voice recognition.
items.evidence: One commenter said fast voice recognition on a Raspberry Pi “alone is worth it”; another wrote, “very nice I love it.”

items.name: Hyperbola GNU/Linux
items.source_story_title: Reviving a 15-year-old netbook with Arch Linux
items.summary: A lightweight GNU/Linux distribution used to keep low-spec netbooks useful.
items.why_included: A commenter supplied detailed first-hand evidence of a workable vintage-netbook setup.
items.evidence: A commenter said their Hyperbola GNU/Linux netbook setup is “really fast” and listed the tools they use on it.

items.name: Q3Edit
items.source_story_title: Show HN: Q3Edit – Edit and play Quake 3 maps in the browser
items.summary: A browser editor and player for Quake 3 maps, valued for hands-on engine exploration.
items.why_included: The project earned direct praise from commenters interested in exploring classic game engines.
items.evidence: A commenter called Q3Edit “awesome” and said they love experimenting with older source-available engines.

items.name: How to Read a Book
items.source_story_title: Judge a book by its first pages
items.summary: Mortimer J. Adler's guide to analytical reading, recommended for its practical reading techniques.
items.why_included: Multiple commenters described it as useful for serious nonfiction reading and gave direct approval.
items.evidence: One commenter said they were “quite impressed”; another wrote, “Can’t recommend the book enough, taught me a lot of tricks.”
